blocks.statement: I can write W = F·d, give units (Joules = N·m), compute work in 1D, and identify cases where work is ZERO even though a force is applied.

blocks.whatWeKnow: Earth's gravity does WORK on 2026-XJ as it falls. That work is what increases the asteroid's energy.
blocks.connection: Work is the bridge between FORCE (Unit 1, Unit 3) and ENERGY (today on). It's how energy MOVES from one object to another.

blocks.markdown:
## Work: the bridge from force to energy

**W = F·d** — work is energy transferred to (or from) an object by a force applied **along its motion**. Units: **Joules**, where 1 J = 1 N·m.

Push a 50-N box 4 m → W = 200 J transferred into the box. Lift a 20-N book 1.5 m → W = 30 J transferred into the book (against gravity).

The catch: work needs **both** a force **and** motion in the direction of that force. Three famous zero-work cases:

| Case | Why W = 0 |
|---|---|
| Push hard on a wall; it doesn't move | d = 0 |
| Carry a book level across the room | force (up) ⊥ motion (sideways) |
| Satellite in circular orbit | gravity ⊥ velocity, always |

terms.definition: Energy transferred to (or from) an object via a force applied along its motion. W = F·d. Pushing a 50-N box 4 m → W = 200 J. Lifting a 20-N book 1.5 m → W = 30 J.
terms.cognate: Sp. trabajo · Pt. trabalho · HC travay

terms.definition: The unit of work and energy. 1 Joule = 1 Newton · 1 meter. Lifting an apple (~1 N) up 1 m takes about 1 J of work. A Joule is small: a snack bar has ~10⁶ J; the asteroid's KE is ~10¹⁷ J — you'll see scientific notation a lot this unit.
terms.cognate: Sp. julio · Pt. joule · HC joul

blocks.title: Work is NOT the same as effort
blocks.markdown: If you push a wall and it doesn't move, you've done ZERO work — even though you got tired. (Your body did work internally, but not on the wall.)

blocks.prompt: You push a 50-N box 4 m across the floor with a constant horizontal force. How much work do you do?
blocks.answer: 200 J of work done on the box.

blocks.prompt: You lift a 20-N book straight up 1.5 m at constant speed. How much work do you do AGAINST gravity?
blocks.givenHint: F = 20 N (the book's weight, lifted along the motion) · d = 1.5 m
blocks.equationHint: Same recipe as the box — force along the motion times distance.

blocks.prompt: THREE ZERO-WORK CASES — for each case, write W and explain in ONE phrase why it's zero. (a) You push hard on a wall; the wall doesn't move. (b) You carry a 20-N book level across a 5-m room. (c) A satellite in CIRCULAR orbit moves around Earth.
blocks.givenHint: For each case ask: is there a d? Is the force ALONG the motion?
blocks.equationHint: W = F·d — lose the d, or lose the 'along the motion', and W = 0.

blocks.title: Both ingredients, or zero
blocks.markdown: Work needs BOTH a force AND motion in the direction of that force. Lose either one → W = 0.

blocks.prompt: You lift a 20-N book 1.5 m, then walk 3 m holding the book at that height. How much work did you do TOTAL on the book? Then complete the frame about the level carry.
blocks.frame: Carrying a book level across the room does NO WORK on the book because the force is ___ and the motion is ___ — and the angle between them is ___°.

blocks.title: Read & practice
blocks.markdown: Open the reader and read **9.1** (work as force × distance, when work is done, the joule). Answer the questions beside the reading as you go.
